coredata.dc:title: Facile fabrication of portable electrospun poly(vinyl alcohol)/sulfur quantum dots film sensor for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+
coredata.dc:creator: Lv, Guangsheng
Dai, Xuanjun
Lu, Guangming
Ye, Lei
Wang, Guan
Zhou, Li
coredata.dc:description: Here, a portable nanofibrous composite film sensor based on biocompatible poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) and fluorescent sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) was prepared by electrospinning method. To endow the PVA/SQDs film sensor with high structural stability, glutaraldehyde was employed as cross-linking agent to react with PVA and SQDs. Thanks to the stabilization of PVA, the PVA/SQDs film showed strong and stable solid-state fluorescence, which overcomes the disadvantage that dried SQDs powder cannot emit fluorescence due to aggregation induced fluorescence quenching effect. In addition, the porous structure of PVA/SQDs film enables it to retain the sensitive fluorescence response ability of SQDs to Fe3+. The PVA/SQDs film exhibited sensitive and selective fluorescence quenching behavior towards Fe3+. The fluorescence intensity of PVA/SQDs film presented good linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0–0.53 mM. The detection limit could reach 0.69 μM, which can well meet the detection demand of Fe3+ in drinking water. Additionally, using PVA/SQDs as a portable sensor to detect Fe3+ in real water sample was examined with satisfactory performance. Considering the simple preparation process and good sensing performance of PVA/SQDs, this paper opens up a new opportunity for using SQDs as a solid sensor to detect metal ions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sulfur quantum dots
Electrospinning
Fluorescence sensor
Film
Detection of Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication of electrospun PVA/SQDs film sensor for fluorescence detection of Fe3+.

(a) TEM image of SQDs. Inset: photograph of aqueous solution of SQDs under 365 nm UV lamp. Representative SEM image (b) and EDX spectrum (c) of PVA/SQDs film. (d) FTIR spectra of pure PVA and PVA/SQDs sample.
Photographs of dried SQDs powder (a,b) and PVA/SQDs film (c,d) under daylight (a,c) and 365 nm UV lamp (b,d).
SEM images of electrospun pure PVA film (a) and PVA/SQDs films with 1.0 wt% (b), 3.0 wt% (c), 5.0 wt% (d), 8.0 wt% (e) and 10.0 wt% (f) of SQDs.

(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of PVA, SQDs and PVA/SQDs. Fluorescence spectra of PVA/SQDs film with various excitation wavelengths (b) or with different SQDs contents (c). Effects of solution pH (d), NaCl concentration (e) and 365 nm UV light illumination (f) on the F/F0 value of PVA/SQDs. F0 represents the fluorescence intensity of PVA/SQDs at pH 7 without addition of NaCl and before UV light illumination. F represents the fluorescence intensity of PVA/SQDs after changing pH or addition of NaCl or illumination with UV light.

(a) Emission spectra of PVA/SQDs film after immersing in different concentration of Fe3+ solutions. The excitation wavelength is 370 nm. (b) Photographs of PVA/SQDs film after immersing in various Fe3+ solutions under 365 nm UV lamp. (c) The relationship between the F/F0 value and the concentration of Fe3+. (d) Linear relationship between the F/F0 value and the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0–0.53 mM.
UV–vis absorption (a) and TRPL (b) spectra of PVA/SQDs composite film before and after immersing in 1 mM of Fe3+ solution. (c) Possible fluorescence quenching mechanism of PVA/SQDs film by Fe3+.
CLSM images of PVA/SQDs film before (a) and after (b) immersing in Fe3+ solution (1.0 mM). Photographs of Fe3+-labeled PVA/SQDs test paper under daylight (c) and 365 nm UV lamp (d).

(a) F/F0 value of PVA/SQDs film against 1.0 mM of different metal ions. (b) Selective fluorescence response of PVA/SQDs film towards 1.0 mM of Fe3+ (red bar), and interference of 1.0 mM of other metal ions with 1.0 mM of Fe3+ (green bars). F0 and F represent the fluorescence intensity of PVA/SQDs film in the absence and presence of metal ions, respectively.
Monitoring of Fe3+ in tap water and river water.

head.ce:title: Facile fabrication of portable electrospun poly(vinyl alcohol)/sulfur quantum dots film sensor for sensitive and selective detection of Fe3+

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of New Processing Technology for Nonferrous Metal and Materials (Ministry of Education), Guangxi Colleges and Universities Key Laboratory of Natural and Biomedical Polymer Materials, College of Materials Science and Engineering, Guilin University of Technology, Guilin, 541004, China

Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Sustainability for Chemicals, Energy and Environment, A*STAR, Singapore, 138634, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Sustainability for Chemicals, Energy and Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Sustainability for Chemicals, Energy and Environment, A*STAR, 138634, Singapore

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Here, a portable nanofibrous composite film sensor based on biocompatible poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) and fluorescent sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) was prepared by electrospinning method. To endow the PVA/SQDs film sensor with high structural stability, glutaraldehyde was employed as cross-linking agent to react with PVA and SQDs. Thanks to the stabilization of PVA, the PVA/SQDs film showed strong and stable solid-state fluorescence, which overcomes the disadvantage that dried SQDs powder cannot emit fluorescence due to aggregation induced fluorescence quenching effect. In addition, the porous structure of PVA/SQDs film enables it to retain the sensitive fluorescence response ability of SQDs to Fe3+. The PVA/SQDs film exhibited sensitive and selective fluorescence quenching behavior towards Fe3+. The fluorescence intensity of PVA/SQDs film presented good linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0–0.53 mM. The detection limit could reach 0.69 μM, which can well meet the detection demand of Fe3+ in drinking water. Additionally, using PVA/SQDs as a portable sensor to detect Fe3+ in real water sample was examined with satisfactory performance. Considering the simple preparation process and good sensing performance of PVA/SQDs, this paper opens up a new opportunity for using SQDs as a solid sensor to detect metal ions.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fe3+ is one of the most crucial trace metal ions in biological system, which plays an important role in many physiological processes, including cell metabolism, enzyme catalysis, oxygen transport, erythropoiesis, and so on [1–3]. Nevertheless, excess intake of Fe3+ may cause diseases such as arthritis and heart failure [4–6]. Therefore, it is of great significance to develop an effective method for detection of Fe3+. In recent years, fluorescence detection technology has been widely used for sensing of Fe3+ due to its merits of high sensitivity, simple operation, and low cost. In order to support fluorescence detection technology, various fluorescent probes have been developed, such as fluorescent dyes [7], polymers [8], carbon dots [9–11], and sulfur quantum dots (SQDs) [12,13]. Among them, metal-free SQDs have attracted particular attention due to their unique fluorescence responsiveness towards Fe3+ and low toxicity [14,15].

Nowadays, SQDs can be synthesized directly from cheap and readily available elemental sulfur [16–19]. They are considered to be one of the most promising fluorescent nanomaterials because of their unique composition, excellent physicochemical properties, low cost and wide application prospect [20,21]. Up to now, the employment of SQDs as a fluorescent probe to detect various analytes (e.g., Cr(VI), Co2+, ascorbic acid and tartrazine) has been widely investigated [22–31]. However, almost all SQDs-based fluorescent probes are in aqueous solution for detection [22–31], which is difficult to carry and complicated to operate, thus limiting the practical applications of SQDs. Additionally, the photostability of SQDs in aqueous solution is generally poor with fluorescence intensity easily attenuated by photo-oxidation. Therefore, it is still a challenge to develop portable and stable SQDs-based sensors for fluorescence detection of specific analytes such as Fe3+. To improve the photostability of SQDs, an effective method is to embed SQDs in polymer matrix. However, the surface of polymer/SQDs composite film prepared by traditional solution blending followed by solvent evaporation is dense and smooth, which makes the embedded SQDs inaccessible to the analytes for fluorescence detection. In contrast, electrospinning is a novel technology that can produce nanoscale fibers by applying an external electric field to the polymer solution [32]. Based on electrospinning technology, fiber films with large surface area and three-dimensional porous structure can be prepared conveniently, which is beneficial to the construction of highly sensitive fluorescence sensors [33–36]. In addition, employing electrospinning technology to fabricate quantum dots/polymer composites with satisfactory sensing properties has been reported [37–39]. As such, it is expected that embedding SQDs into polymer nanofibers by electrospinning technology will not only improve the fluorescence stability of SQDs, but also help to maintain the sensing properties of SQDs.

Sublimed sulfur powder (99.9%), ethylenediamine (EDA, +99.9%), Poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA, degree of hydrolysis is 88%, and average Mw is 85–120 kDa), glutaraldehyde (GA, 50 wt%), ferric chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O, +99.9%), and other metal salts were supplied by Innochem Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China) and used as received. All other reagents are analytical grade and were used directly without further purification. All the water used in the experiment was ultra-pure water (18.2 MΩ).

Highly fluorescent SQDs can be easily synthesized by solvothermal treatment of sublimed sulfur and EDA solution according to our previous report [16]. Typically, 2 g of sublimed sulfur and 20 mL of EDA were mixed in a Teflon-lined hydrothermal reactor. Then the mixture was reacted at 175 °C for 4 h. After reaction and cooling to room temperature, the resulted reaction solution was gradually added to ethanol to precipitate the formed SQDs. The final SQDs were obtained after repeated washing and centrifugation with ethanol.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PVA/SQDs composite film sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The electrospinning solution was prepared by stirring the mixture of PVA (1.0 g), SQDs (0–0.1 g) and water (100 mL) at 90 °C for 1 h. The electrospun PVA/SQDs films with different content of SQDs (0.0 wt%-10.0 wt%) can be easily prepared by adjusting the feeding amount of SQDs. After cooling to 30 °C, a small amount of GA (0.05 g) was added and the mixture was stirred for 1 h to afford homogenous solution. The prepared mixture was then transferred to a syringe containing a 19-gauge needle and electrospun into fine nanofibers. By comparing different electrospinning conditions, it is found that bead-free and uniform PVA/SQDs composite nanofibers can be spun under the conditions of feed rate 6.9 μL/s, feed distance 10 cm and voltage 25 kV. Using tin foil as the receiving plate, the electrospun PVA/SQDs film was obtained.

For fluorescence detection of Fe3+, PVA/SQDs film with 5 wt% of SQDs was chosen as a portable sensor. In a typical procedure, PVA/SQDs film (dimension 0.8 cm × 4.0 cm) was immersed in 3 mL of Fe3+ solution with known concentration for 3 min. Then the composite film was taken out and its fluorescence emission spectrum was recorded with the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. To investigate the selectivity of PVA/SQDs film sensor, similarly, the film was first immersed in 3 mL of aqueous solution containing different metal ions (e.g., Fe2+, Cu2+, Ni2+, Mn2+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Cd2+, Li+, K+, Ag+, and Zn2+) for 3 min. Then the emission intensity of the film was recorded.

The fluorescence emission spectra of PVA/SQDs film were measured by Varian Cary 100 fluorescence spectrometer. UV–visible absorption spectra (Abs) were collected on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-3600). The morphology of PVA/SQDs film was viewed by a S4800 field-emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, Hitachi, Japan). Confocal laser-scanning microscopy (CLSM) observation was performed on an Olympus FV3000 CLSM (Japan). Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) measurement was performed on a JEOL-2010 TEM. Two drops of SQDs solution (1.0 mg/mL) were placed on a copper grid and followed by drying at 50 °C before observation.

The preparation procedure of electrospun PVA/SQDs film sensor is illustrated in Fig. 1. The aminated fluorescent SQDs were synthesized by solvothermal treatment of elemental sulfur-EDA mixture according to our previous report [16]. TEM image of SQDs shows that the spherical SQDs have a narrow size distribution (1.5–3 nm) (Fig. 2 a). The SQDs exhibited excellent water solubility, and their aqueous solution displayed strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 2a). Because PVA is a water-soluble, biodegradable and ecofriendly polymer [40], it was selected as the host polymer to prepare nanofibers by electrospinning method. On the other hand, electrospinning is considered to be one of the simplest approaches to produce long and continuous nanofibers [41]. To prevent the electrospun composite film from being destroyed by water, PVA and SQDs were chemically crosslinked with GA to improve the structural stability of PVA/SQDs film (Fig. 1) [42]. As expected, the PVA/SQDs film became water-insoluble after crosslinking reaction.

SEM image of the PVA/SQDs film shows that the electrospun nanofibers possess smooth surface with diameters in the range of 150–250 nm (Fig. 2b). The nanofibers are arranged together to form a porous film. The corresponding energy-dispersive X-ray (EDX) spectrum in Fig. 2c demonstrated the existence of C, N, O and S elements, which is in accordance with the composition of PVA/SQDs. Compared with the Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum of PVA, the absorption bands centered at 2930 cm−1 and 1101 cm−1 corresponding to the C–H and C–O vibrations, respectively, increased obviously. Meanwhile, a new absorption peak at 1658 cm−1 corresponding to the CN group appeared for the PVA/SQDs sample (Fig. 2d), demonstrated that the aldehyde groups of GA had successfully reacted with the hydroxyl groups of PVA and the amine groups of SQDs.

Notably, the dried SQDs powder cannot emit fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp due to the aggregation induced fluorescence quenching effect (Fig. 3 a and b), which seriously limits the practical application of SQDs in solid state. On the contrary, the dried electrospun PVA/SQDs film can emit strong fluorescence (Fig. 3c and d). This is because embedding SQDs in the PVA matrix can effectively prevent the aggregation of SQDs, thus preserving the fluorescence properties of SQDs. Additionally, the effect of SQDs content on the morphology of electrospun PVA/SQDs mats was investigated. As shown in Fig. 4 , compared with pure PVA nanofibers, the surface morphology and diameter of PVA/SQDs composite nanofibers do not change much with the increase of SQDs content. However, when the content of SQDs was higher than 5.0 wt%, obvious bead-like aggregates appeared in the composite nanofibers, which may be due to the difficulty to form a stable PVA-SQDs electrospinning solution when the concentration of SQDs is too high.

The spectroscopic properties of PVA/SQDs films were studied. As depicted in Fig. 5 a, the PVA/SQDs sample possesses two distinct absorption peaks at 230 nm and 272 nm, which are from PVA and SQDs, respectively. Compared with pure SQDs, the characteristic absorption peak of PVA/SQDs does not shift, indicating that the structural integrity of SQDs is well retained during the electrospinning process. The fluorescence spectra show that the emission wavelength of PVA/SQDs was fixed at 423 nm even when the excitation wavelength was adjusted from 320 nm to 380 nm (Fig. 5b). This excitation-independent emission behavior is similar to that of pure SQDs [16]. Meanwhile, the maximum emission intensity was obtained under 370 nm excitation (Fig. 5b). Additionally, the influence of SQDs content on the fluorescence intensity of PVA/SQDs was investigated. It can be seen that the addition of SQDs can endow PVA with fluorescence characteristics, and the fluorescence intensity of PVA/SQDs film increased obviously with the increase of SQDs content before 5.0 wt% of SQDs content (Fig. 5c). By comprehensively considering the morphology and fluorescence intensity of composite nanofibers, we chose PVA/SQDs with 5 wt% of SQDs content as the representative in the fluorescence detection experiments. It is worth noting that the emission peak of PVA/SQDs is the same as that of pure SQDs (Fig. 5c), which further indicates that the electrospinning method can retain the structure and optical properties of SQDs well. Considering the complexity of practical sensing environment, the optical stability of PVA/SQDs film under various conditions was investigated. As can be seen in Fig. 5d, both in acidic or alkaline environments, the fluorescence intensity of composite film is slightly weakened but still maintains a strong fluorescence stability (>80%). At the same time, the PVA/SQDs film also exhibits stable fluorescence against high ionic strength (e.g., 1.0 M of NaCl) (Fig. 5e). Hence, the PVA/SQDs film holds potential to be utilized in harsh environment.

Good photostability is very important for fluorescent probes, while the photostability of pure SQDs is usually poor, because their surface is easy to be photo-oxidized. Here, the photostability of PVA/SQDs films was evaluated by illumination under 365 nm UV light for different time. The results show that the fluorescence intensity of pure SQDs dropped by 46%, while that of the PVA/SQDs film decreased by only 5% (Fig. 5f) after illumination for 10 min. In addition, it was noted that the fluorescence intensity of the composite film did not decrease significantly after 6 min of illumination. The enhanced photostability of PVA/SQDs film could be attributed to the fact that embedding SQDs into the PVA matrix can effectively prevent the photo-oxidation reaction on the surface of SQDs.

To confirm the potential of PVA/SQDs film as a sensor for fluorescence detection of Fe3+, the emission spectra of composite film before and after immersing in different Fe3+ solutions were monitored. Clearly, the fluorescence intensity of electrospun PVA/SQDs film decreases obviously with increased concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. 6 a). In addition, this fluorescence quenching phenomenon can be clearly observed by naked eyes. As displayed in Fig. 6b, the blue fluorescence of immersed PVA/SQDs film gradually decreased with increased concentration of Fe3+. The quenching efficiency (F/F0, where F0 and F represent the emission intensity of PVA/SQDs film at 423 nm before and after immersing in Fe3+ solution, respectively) is highly related to the concentration of Fe3+ (Fig. 6b and c). Particularly, the F/F0 value shows a good linear correlation with Fe3+ in the range of 0–0.53 mM (Fig. 6d), which provides an opportunity for quantitative sensing of Fe3+. The detection limit of PVA/SQDs film to Fe3+ is calculated to be 0.69 μM (signal-to-noise ratio is 3), which is well below the standard of drinking water in China (0.30 mg/L, about 5.36 μM) [13].

To probe the quenching mechanism, the absorption and time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) spectra of PVA/SQDs film before and after immersing in 1 mM of Fe3+ solution were compared. As depicted in Fig. 7 a, the absorption peak of PVA/SQDs shifts from 272 nm to 293 nm after interaction with Fe3+, indicating the existence of complexation interaction between SQDs and Fe3+. Meanwhile, the fluorescence lifetime of PVA/SQDs film decreased from 9.82 ns to 6.60 ns after immersing in Fe3+ solution (Fig. 7b). Based on the above results, we speculate that the fluorescence response of PVA/SQDs film towards Fe3+ is attributed to electron transfer [43,44]. Firstly, the amine groups on the surface of SQDs may complex Fe3+ [45]. Then the excited state electrons of SQDs (S1) may transfer to the half-filled 3d orbits of Fe3+ (Fig. 7c), resulting in fluorescence quenching of SQDs by the non-radiative electron/hole recombination annihilation [46].

The quenching effect of fluorescent PVA/SQDs film by Fe3+ was also visualized by means of CLSM. In the CLSM image of PVA/SQDs sample (Fig. 8 a), the composite nanofibers display strong blue fluorescence. Upon immersion into Fe3+ solution (1 mM) for 3 min, the fluorescence of the composite nanofibers was quenched significantly (Fig. 8b), which was consistent with the spectral results. To further confirm that the electrospun PVA/SQDs film can be used as a portable Fe3+ sensor, a fluorescent test paper was fabricated. Fig. 8c shows the test paper of electrospun PVA/SQDs film with dendritic shape and flowers. The test paper emitted strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp. When Fe3+ solution (1 mM) was added, the whole test paper appeared to have no color change under daylight, but the fluorescence of flowers with Fe3+ solution was significantly quenched under 365 nm UV lamp (Fig. 8d). These results indicate that electrospun PVA/SQDs film possesses the potential to be employed as a portable sensor for visual detection of Fe3+.

To further assess the selectivity of PVA/SQDs film towards Fe3+, the fluorescence intensity of composite film after immersing in 1.0 mM of other metal ions solutions including Li+, K+, Ag+, Fe2+, Cu2+, Zn2+, Co2+, Mn2+, Cd2+, Mg2+, Ni2+, and Ca2+ was recorded. As depicted in Fig. 9 a, other metal ions presented negligible (e.g., Li+, K+, Ca2+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+ and Zn2+) or slight (e.g., Ag+, Fe2+ and Cu2+) quenching effect. Additionally, the effect of coexisting metal ions on the fluorescence quenching behavior was also investigated. The fluorescence intensity of PVA/SQDs film in single Fe3+ solution and in aqueous mixture of Fe3+ (1.0 mM) and other metal ions (1.0 mM) was determined. As illustrated in Fig. 9b, the coexisting metal ions (e.g., Cd2+ and Fe2+) only exhibit slight quenching effect, further demonstrated the good selectivity of PVA/SQDs film to Fe3+.

To further verify the applicability of PVA/SQDs film in real water samples, the application of PVA/SQDs film in the detection of Fe3+ in tap water and river water was evaluated. The tap water comes from the laboratory and the river water comes from the Lijiang River (Guilin, China). Prior to the test, tap water and river water were centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 10 min and filtered through a 0.45 μm membrane. As shown in Table 1 , the measured Fe3+ concentrations are in good agreement with the spiked values. In addition, the recoveries and relative standard deviation (RSD) value are in the range of 95.4–108.3% and 0.84–1.53%, respectively. Therefore, this electrospun PVA/SQDs film can be used as a portable sensor to detect Fe3+ in real water environment.

In summary, we demonstrate a simple and viable electrospinning method to fabricate a portable and porous PVA/SQDs film sensor. Thanks to the stabilization of PVA, the prepared PVA/SQDs film exhibited strong solid-fluorescence, which overcomes the shortcoming of aggregation quenched fluorescence for dried SQDs powder. In addition, the fluorescence of PVA/SQDs is very stable, even under the conditions of pH change, high ionic strength and UV photobleaching. Moreover, the PVA/SQDs film shows selective fluorescence response towards Fe3+. The detection limit of Fe3+ by PVA/SQDs was calculated to be 0.69 μM, which can well meet the detection requirement of Fe3+ in drinking water. Moreover, we also confirmed that the electrospun PVA/SQDS film can be used as a portable sensor to detect Fe3+ in real water samples.

Guangsheng Lv: Conceptualization, Methodology, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Xuanjun Dai: Methodology, Investigation, Validation. Guangming Lu: Conceptualization, Investigation, Supervision. Lei Ye: Investigation, Validation, Writing – review & editing. Guan Wang: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Li Zhou: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U21A2097), Natural Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2017GXNSFFA198002), Key Laboratory of Non-ferrous Metals and New Materials Processing Technology of Ministry of Education/Guangxi Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Materials and Devices Open Fund (No. 20KF-9), and Bagui Scholar Program of Guangxi Province.

Interfaces 9 (2017) 12663-12672.

Bioelectron. 99 (2018) 303-311.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Preparation of fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for highly selective on-off detection of Fe3+ ions in real samples
